name: UC-0150 — Energy Justice
description: Can the revenues generated by the Slim Strandnet solar cooperative at Living Lab Scheveningen be routed directly to vulnerable households, reducing their energy bills? Testing a mechanism for passive benefit — where the cooperative does the work and the household simply pays less.
challengeOwner: SZW Innovatielab, Municipality of The Hague

0.notes: Project initiated as part of the Slim Strandnet energy justice workstream. Budget secured via Living Lab Scheveningen and EC-DISC.
0.gateNotes: Strategy aligned with municipal energy poverty policy and Slim Strandnet cooperative objectives. Sponsor confirmed at EC-DISC.

thinks: I can't afford solar panels — why should others benefit and not me?
The energy transition feels like something that happens to other people
I worry every month whether I can pay the energy bill
feels: Frustrated by policies that seem to help the wealthy
says: The subsidies are not for people like us
We're renters, we can't install anything
Nobody asked us what we need
does: Doesn't engage with energy cooperative communications
sees: Solar panels appearing on neighbors' roofs
Energy cooperative invitations that feel irrelevant
hears: Friends talking about lower energy bills from solar panels
News about the energy transition being 'for everyone'
Welfare advisors warning about gift rules
pains: Energy bills are the biggest monthly stress
No way to participate in the energy transition
Gift policy means donations may reduce benefits — net zero effect
gains: Lower energy bills without having to do anything
Feeling included in the community's energy future
Financial breathing room for basic needs
needs: Passive benefit — no complex sign-ups or investments required
Legal certainty that benefits won't be offset
A model that works within the welfare system

highHigh.notes: Task owner for experiment — initiated gift policy revision

highHigh.name: Living Lab Scheveningen
highHigh.notes: Energy cooperative providing the surplus revenues

highHigh.notes: Digital innovation centre managing the use case

dmu.decisionMaker: SZW Innovatielab — Social Affairs Innovation Lab
dmu.steeringGroup: Social Affairs + Housing + Legal + Living Lab
dmu.sponsor: Municipality of The Hague, via EC-DISC
dmu.legal: Municipal legal team — gift policy and meldplicht
dmu.privacy: DPIA conducted proactively as design tool
dmu.communications: Communication deliberately deferred to experiment phase

1.notes: Problem analysis identified the giftenbeleid (welfare gift policy) as the central structural barrier. Cooperative surplus mechanism proven conceptually — but legal framework not equipped for social energy models.
1.gateNotes: Problem well understood. The energy transition risks widening inequality — passive benefit model via cooperative surplus offers a viable pathway. Proceed to ideation.

2.notes: Startup in Residence challenge on energy justice preceded this phase — Voorstroom selected as supplier. Core idea: route cooperative surplus to energy bills of vulnerable households via social cooperative.
2.gateNotes: Passive benefit model selected: cooperative does the work, household simply pays less. Social cooperative structure validated conceptually. Proceed to prototyping.

3.notes: Voorstroom platform selected through Startup in Residence programme. Platform proactively audited against ISO 27001 as design benchmark.
3.gateNotes: Prototype validated with Voorstroom platform. Proceed to validation.

privacy.notes: DPIA conducted proactively as design tool — not legally required but used to build compliance into architecture from the ground up.

security.notes: Voorstroom platform audited against ISO 27001 information security standards. Data minimisation mechanisms in place.

data_ai.notes: Minimal data processing. Workflow automation via Voorstroom platform.

info_mgmt.notes: Management plan drafted for cooperative data flows.

procurement.notes: Voorstroom selected through Startup in Residence programme — innovative procurement route.

legal.notes: Giftenbeleid (gift policy) is the central challenge. Under existing rules, cooperative payments to benefit recipients could be classified as income, reducing benefits by the same amount — net zero effect. Meldplicht (reporting obligation) built into design. SZW Innovatielab initiated policy revision.

ethics.notes: Ethics review completed. Passive benefit design minimises burden on vulnerable participants.

4.notes: Validation phase established 5 key findings: (1) Impact indicators: financial + awareness + participation + replicability, (2) DPIA used proactively as design tool, (3) Voorstroom audited against ISO 27001, (4) Communication deliberately deferred to experiment, (5) DMU defined with SZW Innovatielab as task owner. Policy consequence: giftenbeleid revision initiated.
4.gateNotes: All validations complete. Legal risk (giftenbeleid) managed through meldplicht architecture and policy revision process. Viable path to energy justice confirmed. Proceed to experiment.

customTasks.name: Step 1: Define target group and amounts
customTasks.description: Identify which households in need will participate and determine contribution per household.

customTasks.name: Step 2: Configure Voorstroom platform
customTasks.description: Commission the platform to administer cooperative social contributions and track payments.

customTasks.name: Step 3: Onboard target group
customTasks.description: Work with local non-profit to bring participants in — with appropriate information, consent, and care around legal situation.

customTasks.name: Step 4: Process data and execute payment
customTasks.description: Route cooperative financial contribution to participants' energy bills through configured system.

customTasks.name: Step 5: Fulfil meldplicht
customTasks.description: Report donation to relevant municipal authority — formal reporting obligation ensuring correct benefit characterisation.

customTasks.name: Step 6: Reflect and evaluate
customTasks.description: Assess experiment against impact indicators, produce phase report, determine whether to proceed to feasibility.

5.notes: Experiment structured as 6-step process (autumn 2024 – spring 2025): Define target group → Configure Voorstroom → Onboard participants → Execute payments → Fulfil meldplicht → Reflect and evaluate. Parallel tracks: impact measurement and carefully timed communications.

name: UC-0161 — Smart Charging Street
description: Integrating locally generated solar energy, smart charging, shared mobility, and a neighbourhood battery into one system in the Vruchtenbuurt — a residents' initiative redesigning energy, mobility, and public space.
challengeOwner: Mobility Department, Municipality of The Hague

0.notes: QuickScan conducted on 2 August 2023 by EC-DISC and Mobility department. Challenge met conditions for innovation process.
0.gateNotes: QuickScan passed. Residents' initiative from Sterk op Stroom (200+ members) and Vruchtenbuurt Deelt warrants structured innovation approach.

symptom-tree.problem: The local electricity grid and outdoor space are not being optimally used

process: Fixed routes for energy don't match demand patterns
technology: Challenge of realising local energy storage
organization: Challenge of shaping exploitation and ownership of local projects
Energy transition not proceeding fairly — state aid implications
resources: An innovation location needed for grid-conscious charging in The Hague
Standalone charging poles don't invite collective behaviour

highHigh.name: Sterk op Stroom (energy cooperative, 200+ members)
highHigh.notes: Operates collective solar roof, smart network for ~30 homes, 10-year experimental regulation exemption

highHigh.name: EC-DISC
highHigh.notes: Use case management and innovation process

lowHigh.name: Vruchtenbuurt residents
lowHigh.notes: End users and community support base

thinks: Why do charging spots take up so much public space?
Could our solar panels actually power the neighborhood's cars?
Who decides where the charging points go?
feels: Ownership over their neighborhood's energy future
Frustrated by parking pressure from charging infrastructure
Excited about cooperative model but uncertain about complexity
says: We want a say in how our street looks and functions
The energy transition should benefit everyone here
does: Joins Sterk op Stroom or Vruchtenbuurt Deelt
Charges EV at publicly available points without smart scheduling
sees: Solar panels on the collective roof
Shared cars parked in the street
More and more charging poles appearing
hears: News about energy cooperatives and V2G technology
pains: Parking pressure — fully charged cars blocking charging spots
No connection between local solar generation and local EV charging
Complex governance — who owns and operates what?
gains: Lower energy costs through cooperative membership
Cleaner air and less car dependence
A street designed for people, not just cars
needs: Integrated system connecting solar, charging, storage, and sharing
Clear governance: who owns, operates, and benefits
Smart scheduling that matches charging to local energy availability

1.notes: Mapped 30 interconnected problem elements across 7 clusters. 6 problems scored highest (score 4): innovation location needed, residents want space, climate goals not met, local storage challenging, growing privatisation of outdoor space, valuable space underutilised. Focus: 'The local electricity grid and outdoor space are not being optimally used.' Hypothesis: smart charging plaza with shared cars will help. Over 40 stakeholder organisations identified across 6 municipal departments.
1.gateNotes: Problem system comprehensively mapped. Complex (level 3/4), inter-disciplinary (level 3), co-ownership involvement (level 4 — highest), national scale implications. Synergy Hackathon (Feb 2024) with TU Delft, Haagse Hogeschool, and 3 municipalities produced conceptual shift: from charging plaza for cars to charging plaza for people. Proceed to Ideation.

taskNotes.p1-g2-t6: Workshop 1a: Symptom Tree and Values Canvas used. 30 problem elements surfaced and connected as causal network across 7 themes.
taskNotes.p1-g2-t11: Workshop 1b: Problem Focus distilled by consensus. Stakeholder mapping identified 40+ organisations. Empathy mapping for residents conducted.
taskNotes.p1-g3-t2: Problem Characteristics: Complexity 3/4, Dimension Physical & Hybrid, Interdisciplinarity 3, Involvement 4 (Co-ownership), Uncertainty 2, Scale 3 (National), Persistence 3 (Years), Changeability 3.

customTasks.name: Neighbourhood event: Sharing is the New Owning
customTasks.description: Sterk op Stroom and Vruchtenbuurt Deelt organised event (Sept 2024) to broaden support, recruit cooperative members, and gauge appetite for energy sharing and shared mobility.

ideas.text: Smart data system linking car lending with charging scheduling
Fast charging linked to time windows and neighbourhood energy availability
Bi-directional charging (V2G) tied to fixed parking spots
Short-term parking + fast charging at local shops
Energy storage in heat (heat pumps as flexible assets)
Limit parking of fully charged cars to free up charging spots

2.notes: Naming shift: 'Smart Charging Plaza' → 'Smart Charging Street' — Dutch 'plein' (plaza) carried misleading associations. 38 ideas generated and scored. Priorities: Mobility (162pts) >> Grid Congestion (113) >> Data Management (110) >> Space (103) >> Participation (96). Three parallel prototyping tracks identified: (1) Group Contract — governance agreements, (2) Smart Shared Charging Poles — connected to local generation, (3) Space-Friendly Storage — neighbourhood battery with fast/hybrid/bi-directional charging.

taskNotes.p2-g2-t4: Problem Characteristics from Phase 1 used to select methods. Synergy Hackathon format chosen as combined deepening + ideation.
taskNotes.p2-g2-t6: 38 ideas scored by participants. Mobility scored highest (162), then Grid Congestion (113), Data Management (110), Space (103), Participation (96).

name: Smart Waste Collection
description: Exploring sensor-based smart waste collection to optimize routes and reduce costs in residential areas.

0.gateNotes: All preconditions met. Budget confirmed from sustainability fund.

process: Fixed routes don't match actual fill levels
policy: Collection frequency based on historical data
organization: No data-sharing between waste and planning
